title: Fundraising Readiness Radar

introduction.subtitle: Readiness assessment. 6 min.
introduction.description:
Fundraising Readiness Radar helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Narrative & Ambition, Evidence & Traction, Process & Targeting, Deal Readiness. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

The score stays separate from the optional answer-support check, which asks whether your answers are based on opinion, anecdotes, documentation, or system-verified evidence. A high answer should mean the practice is visible, repeatable, and useful in real decisions.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.duration: 8 diagnostic questions plus 4 evidence checks. Approximately 6 min.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How clearly can you explain the problem, customer, and why now in one minute?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Narrative & Ambition: How clearly can you explain the problem, customer, and why now in one minute
conceptHelp.summary: Narrative & Ambition means whether the fundraising story explains the customer, problem, timing, ambition, and why this can become a venture-scale outcome. This question narrows the concept to "How clearly can you explain the problem, customer, and why now in one minute?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Narrative & Ambition matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the investor-facing proof for "How clearly can you explain the problem, customer, and why now in one minute?", who owns it, and how it changes investor targeting, the ask, or the next milestone.

questions.text: How credible is the venture-scale outcome?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Narrative & Ambition; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Narrative & Ambition, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Narrative & Ambition has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Narrative & Ambition is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How well do you understand the economics behind growth?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

contextQuestions.text: What level of evidence supports your answers for Narrative & Ambition?

options.text: Opinion: mostly judgement, memory, or individual perception.
Anecdotal: examples exist, but they are not systematically documented.
Documented: there are current notes, models, policies, metrics, or records.
System-verified: evidence is routinely produced by a governed workflow, system, or review cadence.

conceptHelp.title: Narrative & Ambition
conceptHelp.summary: This question asks how well your answers about Narrative & Ambition are supported. Narrative & Ambition means whether the fundraising story explains the customer, problem, timing, ambition, and why this can become a venture-scale outcome. The goal is learning: separate what you know from what you suspect, remember, or still need to verify.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A result about Narrative & Ambition is more useful when you know whether it rests on opinion, a few examples, current documents, or system-verified evidence. Weak support does not make the answer useless; it tells you what to learn next.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the strongest support you actually have right now. Do not upgrade an answer because the practice feels important, and do not downgrade it if current records or systems really exist.
conceptHelp.example: If your Narrative & Ambition answer is based on a recent metric, policy, deal document, customer record, or operating review, choose documented or system-verified. If it is mostly memory or judgement, choose opinion or anecdotal.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
contextDescription: This step is not scored. It records what your diagnostic answers are based on: opinion, anecdote, documentation, or system-verified evidence. Use it to decide how much to verify the result before acting on it.
